segments.inferno-19-g65: Tal is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' placed at the front for emphasis. Mi is the reflexive/indirect object pronoun ('to/for myself') and precedes the verb in this poetic order. Fec' is an archaic contracted past form (fec' = feci/fece) meaning 'I did/made'; io simply marks the subject 'I' and is added for emphasis.
segments.inferno-19-g66: Quai (a variant of quali/quelli) is an indefinite/relative pronoun introducing a comparison ('such, those who'). Son is the present indicative of essere ('are'). Color is a substantive use meaning 'those people' (lit. 'colors' = 'people'). Che introduces the relative clause and stanno is the present of stare used here as 'stand/are situated'.
segments.inferno-19-g67: Per introduces the reason/ground ('for/because of') and is followed by the infinitive intender ('to understand'); the infinitive after per expresses the fact these people fail to grasp something. Ciò is a demonstrative 'that/what' and ch' (che) is the relative 'that,' with è 'is' forming ch'è. Lor is the enclitic indirect object 'to them,' and risposto is a past participle ('answered') functioning as part of 'what is/has been answered to them.'
segments.inferno-19-g68: Quasi here means 'as if' (introducing a comparison or manner). Scornati is a past participle used adjectivally ('abashed/ashamed'), from an older verb meaning to shame or embarrass; Dante uses it poetically to mark their embarrassed appearance.
segments.inferno-19-g69: E is the coordinating 'and.' Risponder is the present infinitive 'to answer' placed before the finite verb by poetic inversion. Non sanno uses sapere in the present ('they know') + infinitive to express ability; together 'non sanno risponder' means 'they do not know how to answer' or 'they cannot answer.'
segments.inferno-19-g70: "Allor" is a temporal adverb meaning "then/thereupon" placed at the start for narrative effect; the word order is adverb + proper name (subject) + verb. "disse" is the passato remoto (simple past) third person singular of dire, so: "Virgil said:" — the colon introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-19-g71: "Dilli" is an imperative form made by attaching the clitic pronoun li to the imperative di' (from dire), so it means "(you) tell him"; this is a common poetic/colloquial contraction. "tosto" is an adverb (“quickly/immediately”) modifying the imperative.
segments.inferno-19-g72: "Non" negates the clause. "son" is the poetic/clipped form of sono and here is first person singular present "I am" (Italian often omits the subject pronoun). "colui" = "that one / the one"; together: "I am not the one."
segments.inferno-19-g73: This repeats the negation for emphasis: "non son colui che credi." "che" introduces a relative (here operating like English "whom/that"). "credi" is present indicative, second person singular: "you think/believe (me to be)." The object pronoun is omitted but understood ("the one you think [I am]").
segments.inferno-19-g74: "e io rispuosi" places an emphatic pronoun "io" before the passato remoto "rispuosi" (I answered). "come a me fu imposto" is a passive perfect construction: "as to me was imposed" → idiomatically "as I was commanded/told to do." "fu imposto" is passato remoto passive (fu + past participle).
segments.inferno-19-g75: "Per che" here functions as a narrative connective meaning "whereupon/therefore." The clause has subject "lo spirto" and the passato remoto verb "storse" (he twisted/turned). "tutti" intensifies/quantifies "i piedi" (his feet): literally "the spirit turned all his feet (aside)" — a gesture. Word order is typical narrative: connective + subject + modifier + verb + object.
segments.inferno-19-g76: "poi" is a simple temporal/discourse adverb meaning "then/afterwards," linking the previous physical gesture to the next action; it sits alone followed by a comma.
segments.inferno-19-g77: "sospirando" is a gerund (present participle) meaning "sighing," indicating an action simultaneous with speaking. "e con voce di pianto" is an instrumental/adjunct prepositional phrase: "and with a voice of weeping/lament," describing the manner of speaking.
segments.inferno-19-g78: "mi disse" is the standard clitic + verb ordering for indirect objects in Italian: mi (to me) + disse (he said, passato remoto). The clause introduces direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-19-g79: "Dunque" is a discourse marker meaning "so/then." Here "che" introduces an interrogative nominal clause meaning "what" ("what do you ask [of me]?"). "a me" emphasizes the indirect object "to me," and "richiedi" is present indicative, 2nd person singular: "do you ask/request." Together: "So, what do you ask of me?"
segments.inferno-19-g80: This is a conditional clause introduced by "Se" (if). "di saper" is the preposition + infinitive construction meaning "to know;" "ch'i' sia" is the subordinate clause ("who I am") with "sia" in the present subjunctive (ch' = che + io). "ti cal cotanto" is an impersonal/ethical construction: "cal" (calere) takes the dative "ti" to mean "it matters to you" and "cotanto" intensifies it ("so much"). Word order is older/poetic but the components are: if + (it is important for you) to know who I am.
segments.inferno-19-g81: This result clause explains the reason: "che" = "that." "tu abbi ... corsa" uses "abbi" (present subjunctive/archaic auxiliary of avere) plus the past participle "corsa" to form a perfect sense: "you have run." Here it means "so that you have run along the bank (la ripa) for that reason." The unusual word order (object after participle) reflects poetic diction.
segments.inferno-19-g82: "sappi" is the imperative (second person singular) of sapere: "know (this)!" It introduces the information that follows. "ch'i' fui vestito" is a narrative perfect in passato remoto: "that I was clothed/dressed." "del gran manto" is a genitive phrase "of the great mantle." Note some of the lemma senses provided are mismatched to the forms here (e.g. "sappi" is imperative, not "I know").
segments.inferno-19-g83: "e veramente fui figliuol de l'orsa" is coordinate: "and truly I was a son of the she‑bear." "fui" is passato remoto "I was;" "figliuol" is the noun "son/child." The phrase is literal/figurative identification, with the preposition "de" = "of."
segments.inferno-19-g84: "cupido" is used predicatively here ("desirous/eager") and modifies the preceding identification: "so desirous (am I) to advance the little bears." "sì" = "so" / "so much;" "per avanzar" is a purpose phrase with the infinitive "avanzar" = "to advance/promote;" "li orsatti" is the direct object "the little bears/young bears." The clause states motive: great desire to promote the orsatti.
segments.inferno-19-g85: "che" introduces the result/consequence: "so that..." "sù l'avere" is idiomatic/poetic = "for the sake of having/possessions" (sù = up/over, l'avere = possessions). "e qui me misi in borsa" has "misi" (passato remoto of mettere, I put) with the clitic "me" (I put myself) and the prepositional phrase "in borsa" (into the purse/bag) — literally "I put myself into the bag/purse," i.e. sold or put myself up for gain. Note: the provided sense list for "misi" does not include "to put," so that particular sense is marked -1.
segments.inferno-19-g57: Ed = 'and' (simple conjunction). El is an archaic/neapolitan subject pronoun/article used here as the masculine personal subject 'he'. Gridò is passato remoto (third person singular) of gridare, 'he cried/out' or 'he shouted' and completes the main clause announcing the speech.
segments.inferno-19-g58: This is a direct quotation. Se’ is the contracted/poetic form of sei (second-person singular present indicative of essere) meaning 'you are'; tu is the explicit subject pronoun used for emphasis. Già = 'already' (an adverb of time). Costì is an adverb meaning 'there, over there'; ritto is an adjective meaning 'upright/standing' placed after the subject phrase and describing the state of 'you'.
segments.inferno-19-g59: This segment repeats the exact question for emphasis or echo — same grammar as the previous line: Se’ (you are) + tu (you) + già (already) + costì (there) + ritto (standing). The repetition is a rhetorical/poetic device, not a different grammatical construction.
segments.inferno-19-g60: A vocative: the proper name Bonifazio is used to address the person directly. The question mark shows the name is part of the preceding question (direct address).
segments.inferno-19-g61: Di parecchi anni is an adverbial measure phrase meaning 'by several years' (Di expresses the extent/measure). Mi is the indirect-object clitic 'to me' (dative). Mentì is passato remoto, third person singular of mentire here meaning 'lied' or 'deceived'. Lo scritto is the nominative subject 'the writing/document' (lo = masculine article + scritto used as a noun); word order places the measure phrase fronted for emphasis and the subject after the verb.
segments.inferno-19-g62: Se’ tu = 'are you' (contracted sei + subject pronoun for emphasis). Sì tosto = 'so soon/so quickly' (sì intensifies tosto). Di quell’ aver sazio: sazio is an adjective 'sated/satisfied' that takes di + noun (sazio di = 'satisfied with'); di + quell' aver = 'with that possession/wealth' (aver is an archaic noun-like use from avere meaning 'property' or 'goods'). Per lo qual = 'by/for which' (introduces a relative clause giving the reason or cause). Non temesti — here the expected sense is 'did not fear' (past tense), but the supplied sense list for temesti doesn't include 'fear' so the morphological note: temesti is passato remoto second person singular of temere (to fear) functioning in the relative clause. Tòrre a 'nganno la bella donna = tòrre (infinitive/archaic form 'to take' or 'to remove') + a 'nganno (a + inganno = 'by trick/through deception') + la bella donna (direct object 'the fair lady'); the infinitive tòrre depends on the modal sense introduced by the relative clause (you did not fear to take...).
segments.inferno-19-g63: E poi = 'and then/afterwards' linking the successive action. Di farne is the infinitive construction: di + fare + -ne (ne refers back to 'her' or 'of her', so farne = 'to make of her' or 'to do to her'). Strazio is a noun meaning 'torment/torture/devastation' and is the object/complement of farne: 'to make of her torment' i.e. to mutilate, ravage, or cruelly treat her. The clause as a whole asks if he was not afraid to take her by deceit and then to torment her.
segments.inferno-19-g64: A single punctuation mark: the period/full stop ending the speech or the printed line. No lexical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-19-g1: A vocative opening: "O" is the interjection addressing someone; "Simon mago" is a name + title in the vocative ("mago" = Magus/title). The order is straightforward vocative address.
segments.inferno-19-g2: "o miseri seguaci" is another vocative address ("O wretched followers"). The relative particle "che" begins a clause modifying "seguaci" ("who..."). "le cose di Dio" is a noun phrase: definite article + noun + prepositional phrase "of God."
segments.inferno-19-g3: Relative clause: "che di bontate deon essere spose" = "which/that, of goodness, must be spouses." "deon" is an archaic form of "devono" (they must/ought to) and takes the infinitive "essere"; "spose" is a predicate adjective/nominal (feminine plural) agreeing with "le cose."
segments.inferno-19-g4: Contrast/accusation: "e voi rapaci per oro e per argento avolterate." "voi" (you) + adjective "rapaci" (rapacious/greedy). "per oro e per argento" gives the means/motive. "avolterate" is a past participle meaning "prostituted/adulterated" and agrees in gender/number with "le cose" (feminine plural), so Dante places it with the implied object "them".
segments.inferno-19-g5: "Or convien che per voi suoni la tromba" is an impersonal construction: "convien" (it is necessary/fitting) + subordinate introduced by "che". Because "convien" is impersonal, the verb in the subordinate "suoni" appears in the subjunctive (3rd sing.). "per voi" indicates for whose sake the trumpet must sound.
segments.inferno-19-g6: "però che ne la terza bolgia state" gives the reason: "però che" here has a causal value (archaic, "because/inasmuch as"); "ne" = "in" (old use), "la terza bolgia" = "the third bolgia/ditch," and "state" is 2nd person plural present of "stare/essere" (you are/stand).
segments.inferno-19-g7: "Già eravamo" = "We had already (been)..."; "eravamo" is the imperfect of "essere" used here as auxiliary anticipating the past participle that follows (a periphrastic past: "eravamo montati").
segments.inferno-19-g8: "a la seguente tomba" = "to the next/following tomb." "a" marks motion towards or position; the phrase functions as the goal/destination of the climbing.
segments.inferno-19-g9: "montati de lo scoglio" completes the periphrasis: past participle "montati" (we had mounted/climbed) with "de lo scoglio" = "from the rock." "in quella parte" = "into that part/side," and the relative "ch’ a punto sovra mezzo ’l fosso piomba" describes that part — "which at the very point plunges/swoops over the middle of the ditch." "piomba" is 3rd sing. present (plunge/descend).
segments.inferno-19-g10: Vocative address to the abstract personification "somma sapïenza" ("highest wisdom"). "O" is the interjection and the adjective "somma" qualifies "sapïenza."
segments.inferno-19-g11: "quanta è l’arte che mostri in cielo" = "how great is the art/skill that you show in heaven." "quanta" is an exclamatory/relative expression "how much/what a," "è" is present indicative, and "che" introduces the relative clause. Note that "mostri" here is a verb (you show) addressing the Sapïenza.
segments.inferno-19-g12: "in terra e nel mal mondo" lists the spheres where that art is seen: "in terra" (on earth) and "nel mal mondo" (in the evil/bad world). "mal" qualifies "mondo."
segments.inferno-19-g13: "e quanto giusto tua virtù comparte!" — "e quanto" = "and how/what a," "giusto" = "just/right," "tua virtù" = "your power/virtue," and "comparte" is here a verb (3rd sing.) meaning "distributes/shares out." The phrase is an exclamation praising the just distribution of divine power.
segments.inferno-19-g14: "Io vidi" = simple past (perfect) "I saw." "per le coste e per lo fondo" gives the location/extent (along the slopes and the bottom). "piena la pietra livida di fóri" = "the livid stone full of holes" — "piena" (full) precedes the noun in a poetic word order and the genitive/partitive follows.
segments.inferno-19-g15: "d’un largo" = "of one breadth/width" (describing the holes). "tutti e ciascun era tondo" = "all and each was round" — "tutti e ciascun" emphasizes that every single hole was round; "era" is 3rd sing. imperfect/past (predicate about each hole).
segments.inferno-19-g29: Chi è colui is an interrogative phrase: Chi ('who') + è (present indicative of essere, 'is') + colui ('that one'). Word order is Subject‑Complement (Chi = interrogative subject), and colui is the demonstrative pronoun identified by the question.
segments.inferno-19-g30: maestro is a vocative: the speaker addresses his guide directly. It is simply a noun in an address position and does not affect the clause's verb form.
segments.inferno-19-g31: che introduces a relative clause ('who/that') modifying colui. si cruccia is reflexive (si + verb) meaning 'is distressed/worries himself' in the present; guizzando is a present participle meaning 'writhing/darting about' and modifies the verb; più ... che introduces a comparative ('more ... than'); li altri suoi consorti = 'his other companions' (li = definite article + altri 'other' + suoi 'his' + consorti 'companions').
segments.inferno-19-g32: diss' io is an archaic/synoptic past report: diss' is a contracted past tense of dire (disse) 'said' placed before the subject io (inversion of verb and pronoun for poetic effect).
segments.inferno-19-g33: This is the second interrogative clause joined by e ('and'). cui is a relative/interrogative pronoun here with a sense of 'in whom' or 'to whom'; più is comparative 'more'; roggia is from roggiare ('redden, make red') used adjectivally to describe the flame as 'redder' or 'more reddening'; fiamma is 'flame'; succia is present subjunctive/indicative form of succiare (here 3rd person singular) meaning 'sucks'/'drinks in'—the image is the flame consuming or sucking at someone. The clause asks into whom the redder flame sucks more.
segments.inferno-19-g34: A single punctuation mark: the sentence here consists only of the closing period that ends the preceding quoted question. There are no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-19-g35: Ed = and (conjunction). elli is the pronoun 'egli' (he) used as the subject; a me = 'to me' marks the indirect object/recipient of speech. The sequence marks reported speech: 'And he to me:' introduces the quotation.
segments.inferno-19-g36: Se tu vuo' (archaic spelling of vuoi / vuoi) introduces a conditional 'If you want'; ch'i' is contraction of che + io ('that I'); ti porti uses the present subjunctive porti because volere + che requires the subjunctive and it expresses the wished action 'that I carry/lead you'; là giù per quella ripa 'down there along that bank/edge' specifies direction; che più giace is a relative clause 'which lies lower/lowest' (più + giace = lies more/lowest).
segments.inferno-19-g37: da lui saprai: da + lui = 'from him' and saprai is future of sapere 'you will know/learn'; di sé = 'about himself' (reflexive pronoun); e de' suoi torti = 'and of his wrongs/misdeeds' (de' = of the, suoi = his, torti = wrongs). The verb saprai is future and promises the listener information to be learned from that person.
segments.inferno-19-g38: E io simply introduces the speaker's reply: 'And I' (subject pronoun io 'I' emphasized by placement after the conjunction).
segments.inferno-19-g39: Tanto m'è bel is an idiomatic inverted construction: Tanto = 'so/as much', m' (mi) = indirect object 'to me', è (present of essere) + bel (short form of bello) = 'is pleasing/pleasant'. Together, 'It pleases me so much' or 'That is as pleasing to me'.
segments.inferno-19-g40: quanto a te piace is a comparative/complement clause: quanto...piace = 'as much as pleases'; a te = 'to you' (indirect object). Literally 'as much as it pleases you.'
segments.inferno-19-g41: tu se' segnore is a plain declarative: tu (you) + se' (colloquial/poetic contraction of sei, 'are') + segnore (lord/master). The inversion and short forms are typical of Dante's diction.
segments.inferno-19-g42: e sai ch'i' non mi parto dal tuo volere: e sai = 'and you know'; ch'i' = che io ('that I') — che + subject pronoun is a frequent contracted form in poetry; non mi parto = 'I do not depart' (present indicative, reflexive mi); dal tuo volere = 'from your will' (dal = da + il). The clause asserts the speaker's obedience to the addressee's will.
segments.inferno-19-g43: e sai quel che si tace: e sai = 'and you know'; quel che = 'that which' or 'what'; si tace is an impersonal/passive present of tacere 'is kept silent/one keeps silent', so the phrase means 'and you know what is left unspoken.'
segments.inferno-19-g44: Allor is an adverb meaning 'then/thereupon'. venimmo is the passato remoto, 1st person plural of venire ('we came/reached'). The prepositional phrase in su l’argine quarto literally combines in + su to mean 'up onto' (in su), followed by the article-contracted l' + argine ('the embankment') and the postpositive adjective quarto ('fourth').
segments.inferno-19-g45: Volgemmo e discendemmo are two passato remoto verbs in coordinate structure ('we turned and we descended'). A mano stanca is an idiomatic instrumental/phrasing ('with a weary hand' or 'with weary hands') where a + noun expresses manner. Là giù is an adverbial pair ('down there'); nel fondo = in + il + fondo ('in the bottom'); foracchiato and arto are postpositive adjectives modifying fondo (foracchiato = 'riddled/holed', arto = archaic adjective 'narrow').
segments.inferno-19-g46: Lo buon maestro uses the archaic article lo before an adjective + noun ('the good master'). ancor (ancora) = 'still/yet'. de la sua anca = 'from his hip' (de = from). The negative clause non mi dipuose is passato remoto, 3rd person singular (dipuose) with the clitic mi placed between negation and verb: 'he did not set me down/put me off.' Dipuose is an archaic past form meaning 'set down/laid aside.'
segments.inferno-19-g47: Sì here functions as 'thus/so'. mi giunse is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of giungere with clitic mi ('he/it reached me' or 'he brought me to'); al rotto = a + il + rotto ('to the broken/ruptured part' or 'to the breach'); di quel che si piangeva con la zanca is a relative clause modifying 'quel' ('of that one who was lamenting himself with the leg'), where si piangeva is reflexive imperfect 'was lamenting' and con la zanca indicates 'with/by the leg/thigh.'
segments.inferno-19-g48: O qual che se’ is an exclamatory address: 'O whatever you are' (qual che se' = 'whatever you are', where se' is an archaic contracted form of sei = 'you are'). The following che introduces a relative clause: 'who/that (hold) ...' '’l di sù tien di sotto' literally 'the day above holds below' — tien is present indicative 3rd person singular of tenere ('holds/keeps'), sù = 'above', sotto = 'below'; overall this is a compressed, poetic description addressed to the being they address.
segments.inferno-19-g49: anima is 'soul'. trista should be read as the adjective 'sad' (the supplied lexical entry appears mistaken), so 'anima trista' = 'sad soul'. come introduces a simile 'like'. pal commessa is a terse, figurative phrase: pal = 'stake/pole' and commessa is a past-participle/adjectival use meaning 'joined/fastened' — 'like a stake fastened' (a graphic image of how the soul lamented).
segments.inferno-19-g50: comincia’ is a contracted, poetical past form meaning 'I began' (cominciai), with the subject pronoun io used after the verb for emphasis. a dir is the infinitival complement 'to say' (a + infinitive).
segments.inferno-19-g51: se puoi is a conditional request 'if you can' (se = 'if', puoi = 2nd person singular present of potere 'you can'). Here it invites the addressee to reply if he is able. Note: the supplied sense list earlier marked se as 'you are' in another line; here se functions as the conjunction 'if.'
segments.inferno-19-g52: fa is an imperative/form of fare used here as a hortative ('do/make' = 'give/say'), a common archaic/poetic imperative form (fa instead of modern fai). motto is a noun meaning 'a word/utterance' — so fa motto = 'say a word' or 'make some reply.'
segments.inferno-19-g53: Io stava uses stava (imperfect of stare) meaning 'I stood/was standing' — a past, ongoing or habitual sense. come introduces a simile: 'as/like'. '’l frate che confessa' = 'the friar who confesses/hears confession' — here confessa means 'hears confession' (the priest hearing the penitent). lo perfido assessin = 'the treacherous assassin', with lo as the definite article.
segments.inferno-19-g54: che is a relative pronoun linking to the following clause ('who...'). Here it simply introduces the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.inferno-19-g55: poi ch’è fitto is a temporal/circumstantial clause: poi = 'then/afterwards'; ch’ = che; è is 'is'; fitto is a past-participle/adjectival use meaning 'fixed/settled/fastened' — so 'after he/it is fixed' or 'once it is set.'
segments.inferno-19-g56: richiama is present (or historical present) of richiamare 'calls back/recalls' (here 'calls back' or 'recalls him'); lui is the clitic/direct object 'him'. per che is a causal/resultive connector ('because/so that'), and la morte cessa = 'death ceases' (cessa = 'ceases/stops'). The phrase describes the priest's act of calling the penitent back so that death's effect is ended.
segments.inferno-19-g16: Non mi parean = literal word order 'not to me seemed' — parean is the archaic imperfect 3rd plural of parere (they seemed), with the indirect object pronoun mi. 'men ampi né maggiori' is a coordinated comparative: men = 'less', ampi = 'wide/ample', né maggiori = 'nor larger/greater'. The first che functions as 'than' introducing the comparison, and the second che begins a relative clause (che son) 'which are'; son is present indicative 'are'. 'Nel mio bel San Giovanni' is a locative phrase, where bel is the usual apocopated bello before the name.
segments.inferno-19-g17: Fatti is a past participle used adjectivally ('made/built') agreeing with the implied antecedent 'que'' (those). 'per loco d’i battezzatori' indicates the place/function: 'at the place of the baptizers' (d’i = di + i, 'of the'). 'Per' here has a purposive/locative sense ('for/at').
segments.inferno-19-g18: L'un de li quali = 'one of those which/whom': l'un is 'the one'/'one', de li quali is the prepositional phrase 'of whom/which' referring back to the buildings (or persons) just mentioned. Quali is the plural relative 'which/whom'.
segments.inferno-19-g19: Ancor non è molt' anni is an idiom meaning 'it is not many years (since)' — i.e. 'not long ago'. Ancora/non yet/not long; è is impersonal here ('there are/it is (since)'); molt' is the elided form of molti.
segments.inferno-19-g20: Rupp' io is the poetical past tense 'ruppi' (I broke) with the apocopated form used for the verse; the subject is io. The implied object is 'l'un (one of them)'. 'Per un che dentro v’annegava' = 'on account of / because of one who was drowning inside': per = 'because of' here, v’ (vi) = 'in it/there', and annegava is imperfect 'was drowning'.
segments.inferno-19-g21: E questo sia suggel uses sia (present subjunctive) in an optative/result sense: 'and let this be a seal/token'. Suggel (suggello) is a noun 'seal' or 'proof'. The subordinate clause ch’ogn’ omo sganni = 'that every man be/May be mistaken' uses ch’ = that and ogn' omo (each man) as subject; sganni is a verb form implying subjunctive (may be mistaken/misled) describing the effect of the 'suggel'.
segments.inferno-19-g22: Fuor de la bocca a ciascun = 'out of the mouth of each [one]'. Soperchiava is imperfect 3rd singular of soperchiare meaning 'overhung/overflowed/covered' and takes as its (logical) subject the following phrase 'li piedi e de le gambe' (the feet and the legs). D’un peccator is a genitive/possessive 'of a sinner' specifying whose feet. Infino al grosso = 'up to the calf/upper part'.
segments.inferno-19-g23: E l’altro dentro stava = 'and the other (person) was inside'. Stava is the imperfect of stare ('was situated/was standing'), describing the ongoing position of the other person with his body inside while the feet projected out.
segments.inferno-19-g24: Le piante here means 'the soles' (of the feet) rather than 'plants'; erano is imperfect 'were', and accese is past participle/adjective 'alight/aflame'. 'A tutti' = 'on every one' (on all of them) and intrambe = 'both' (both soles).
segments.inferno-19-g25: Per che introduces a result clause ('so that/therefore'), sì forte = 'so strongly/violently', and guizzavan is the imperfect 3rd plural of guizzare 'they writhed/darted/flickered'. Le giunte = 'the joints' is the plural subject of guizzavan.
segments.inferno-19-g26: Che spezzate averien ritorte e strambe is a result/conditional: che = 'that'; spezzate (feminine plural) = 'if they were broken' modifying giunte; averien is the archaic conditional auxiliary (avrebbero) 'would have'; ritorte = 'twisted back' (past participle) and strambe = 'crooked/askew' describing the hypothetical outcome had the joints been broken.
segments.inferno-19-g27: Qual suole ... muoversi = a simile introduced by Qual ('as/just as') with suole ('is wont') governing the infinitive muoversi ('to move itself'), so: 'as the flaming of oiled things is wont to move up...'. Il fiammeggiar is a nominalized infinitive 'the flaming/flickering'; de le cose unte = 'of the oiled/greasy things'; pur su per la strema buccia = 'even/upwards along the outermost skin/surface' (pur = even/still, strema = outermost, buccia = surface/skin).
segments.inferno-19-g28: Tal era lì = 'such was it there' (tal = such, era = was, lì = there). 'Dai calcagni a le punte' = 'from the heels to the tips' (dai = da + i 'from the', calcagni = heels, a le punte = to the tips/toes) — completes the simile by specifying extent.
segments.inferno-19-g129: Punctuation only: a period marking the end of the speech/sentence.
segments.inferno-19-g130: "E" is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. "Mentr'" is an elided form of mentre ('while'), followed by the subject pronoun "io". "Li" is a clitic pronoun standing for the indirect/direct object previously mentioned (here 'to him'/'to them'); it precedes the verb as normal in Italian. "Cantava" is the imperfect indicative of cantare ('I was singing/used to sing'), giving a continuous or repeated past action. "Cotai" is an archaic demonstrative meaning 'such/those', and "note" is plural 'notes/sounds/lines'; the order is standard object clitic + verb + object phrase common in older/poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-19-g131: This is a nominative/absolute phrase listing two possible motivators: "o ira o coscïenza" ('either anger or conscience'). "Che" introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun phrase. The clitic "’l" (from lo) stands for 'him' or 'it' and precedes the verb. "Mordesse" is the imperfect subjunctive of mordere ('might bite/gnaw/sting') used here in a relative clause to express the cause or condition (a subdued/potential action), rather than a straightforward indicative.
segments.inferno-19-g132: "Forte" here functions adverbially ('forcefully/strongly'). "Spingava" is the imperfect indicative of spingere ('he/it was pushing' or 'kept pushing'), describing a continuing or repeated action in the past. "Con ambo le piote" is a prepositional phrase: "con" + "ambo" ('both') + feminine plural article "le" + "piote" (archaic noun 'soles' of the feet or similar). The whole phrase means 'he was pushing hard with both soles/with both feet.'
segments.inferno-19-g133: "I'" is an archaic contraction of io ('I'). "Credo ben" is 'I well/firmly believe' (ben intensifies credo). "Ch'" is the clitic of che ('that'); "al" is the contraction of a + il ('to the') and introduces the indirect object "mio duca" ('my guide', here Virgil). "Piacesse" is the imperfect subjunctive of piacere; in older/poetic usage it appears in subordinate clauses after verbs of belief to report what one believes was pleasing — functionally equivalent here to 'that it pleased my guide.'
segments.inferno-19-g134: This whole clause gives the reason. "Con" ('with') + "sì contenta labbia" ('so/content a expression') — "sì" = 'so', "contenta" = 'content/pleased' and "labbia" is an archaic noun meaning 'countenance/appearance'. "Sempre attese lo suon" uses "attese" (past tense of attendere) meaning 'he listened/paid attention' (here a past simple/preterite sense in narrative). "De/le parole vere espresse" is a noun phrase 'to the sound of the words truly expressed' ("de" = of, "le parole" = the words, "vere espresse" = truly/faithfully expressed). Word order places the descriptive phrase after the verb, typical of poetic diction.
segments.inferno-19-g135: "Però" means 'therefore' or 'then'. "Con ambo le braccia" is a prepositional phrase 'with both arms' ("ambo" = both, "le braccia" = the arms). "Mi" is the first-person clitic object 'me' and precedes the verb in keeping with standard clitic placement. "Prese" is the passato remoto (simple past) of prendere ('he took/seized'), narrating a single past action: 'he took me.'
segments.inferno-19-g136: This temporal clause begins with "e poi che" ('and then/after that') introducing what follows. "Tutto su mi s’ebbe al petto" contains the reflexive clitic "s'" attached to the auxiliary of avere: "s'ebbe" (si ebbe) literally 'he had (him)self' meaning he held me; "tutto su" ('all up/on top') + "mi" 'me' + "al petto" 'to/against his chest' — idiomatically 'he had me all up against his breast.' Note that the clitic "mi" appears before the compounded verb "s'ebbe."
segments.inferno-19-g137: "Rimontò" is the passato remoto of rimontare ('he remounted/went back up'), a perfective past describing the completed action of climbing back up. "Per la via" ('by the way') is followed by "onde" used as a relative/adverbial 'whence' or 'from which' (archaic/poetic usage). "Discese" here refers to the earlier descent ('he had gone down'); the verb form is nominalized in the senses list but functions here as a verb of descent — the whole phrase: 'by the way from which he had descended.'
segments.inferno-19-g138: Né is the negative conjunction 'nor' introducing a continuation of negation. Si stancò is passato remoto (stancarsi) with the reflexive clitic si — 'he grew tired' or literally 'he made himself tired'. D’avermi is the contraction of di + avermi: the infinitive avere + enclitic mi ('to have me') and here means 'to have/hold me'; a sé = 'to himself' (a + sé). Distretto is an adjective/participial form (from distrettare) meaning 'bound/tightly held' and modifies the implied object (me). The whole clause means 'Nor did he tire of holding me close to him.'
segments.inferno-19-g139: Sì here is 'so/thus' and men is the adverb 'less' (archaic men = meno); portò is passato remoto of portare 'he carried/bore'. Sovra 'l colmo = 'over the crown/summit' (sovra + definite article l'); de l'arco = archaic di + l' 'of the arch'. The relative clause che dal quarto al quinto argine è tragetto modifies 'arco': dal...al... = 'from the fourth to the fifth (embankment)' and è tragetto uses essere + noun/participle 'tragetto' meaning 'spanned/crossed'. Together: 'and so he carried me not so high above the crown of the arch that is spanned from the fourth to the fifth embankment.'
segments.inferno-19-g140: Quivi is an adverb meaning 'there'. Soavemente (adverb) = 'gently/softly' describing manner. Spuose is passato remoto of the poetic/archaic verb sporre (here spelled spuorre in the lemma) meaning 'he set down' or 'laid down' (common poetic usage). Il carco = 'the load/burden' (il + carco). The clause literally: 'There he gently set down the load.'
segments.inferno-19-g141: Soave repeats the manner 'gentle' and functions as a comment on the action. Per here means 'because of' or 'on account of' (giving reason). Lo scoglio = 'the crag/rock'; sconcio modifies it as 'unsightly/awkward/rough' and ed is the form of e used before a vowel ('and'); erto = 'steep'. Che sarebbe is a relative clause with the conditional sarebbe (sarebbe = 'would be') introducing the consequence: a le capre = 'for the goats' (a + le) and duro varco = 'a hard passage' (adjective + noun). Altogether: 'gentle because of the rough and steep crag that would be a hard crossing for goats.'
segments.inferno-19-g142: Indi is an adverb 'then/thence' indicating sequence. Un altro vallon is the subject phrase: un (indefinite article) + altro ('another') + vallon ('large valley/ravine'). Mi is the dative/clitic pronoun 'to me' (the person to whom it is revealed). Fu is passato remoto, 3rd person singular of essere used here in a passive construction ('was'). Scoperto is the past participle 'revealed/uncovered' agreeing with vallon in the passive phrase; together mi fu scoperto = 'was revealed to me.'
segments.inferno-19-g111: E se non fosse ... introduces a concessive conditional ('And if it were not that ...'). Fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used in the fixed phrase 'se non fosse' meaning 'if it were not (that)'; ch’ is the contraction of che ('that'); ancor = 'still'. Lo mi vieta places the clitic lo ('it') before mi ('me')—an acceptable older/poetic order—followed by the present indicative vieta ('forbids'), so literally 'it still forbids it to me'. La reverenza de le somme chiavi is the nominative subject of vieta: 'the reverence of the supreme keys'; che tu tenesti ne la vita lieta is a relative clause ('which you held in your happy life') where tenesti is the past tense 'you held' and ne + la vita = 'in (your) life'.
segments.inferno-19-g112: Io userei parole ancor più gravi is a single clause where userei is the conditional of usare ('I would use'); io is emphatic subject pronoun. Parole = 'words'; ancor più gravi = 'even more serious/weighty' (ancor = still/yet, più gravi = 'more grave/serious'). The clause states what the speaker would do if not restrained by reverence.
segments.inferno-19-g113: Ché begins a causal clause ('for/because'). La vostra avarizia is the subject ('your greed'); il mondo is the direct object ('the world'); attrista is a present indicative verb meaning 'makes sad' or 'saddens'. The clause gives the reason for the harsher words the speaker would otherwise use.
segments.inferno-19-g114: Two gerunds (calcando, sollevando) describe concurrent actions of your greed: calcando = 'treading on' or 'trampling', i.e. oppressing; sollevando = 'raising' or 'lifting up'. I buoni (the good) and i pravi (the wicked/depraved) are their respective objects: 'trampling the good and uplifting the wicked.' The verb is implied to be performed by the avarizia from the previous clause.
segments.inferno-19-g115: Di voi = 'concerning you' or 'of you'; pastor is vocative or apposition 'shepherd' (here a spiritual shepherd); s’accorse is si + accorse, the reflexive perf. of accorgersi meaning 'became aware' or 'took note' and il Vangelista is the subject performing the noticing. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'Of you the Evangelist took note.'
segments.inferno-19-g116: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when/whenever'. Colei che siede sopra l’acque = 'she who sits upon the waters'; siede is present indicative 'sits/is seated' and sopra l'acque uses plural 'waters'. Puttaneggiar is an infinitive used predicatively meaning 'to prostitute herself / to act as a harlot'; coi regi 'with the kings' (coi = con + i). A lui fu vista is a passive construction ('was seen by him' or 'was seen by him' — here 'to him' likely refers back to the Evangelist), with fu vista = perfect passive 'was seen'.
segments.inferno-19-g117: Quella che ... nacque is another relative-clause description: quella = 'that one' (the prostitute figure), che con le sette teste nacque = 'who was born with seven heads' where nacque is the past historic/passive 'was born/arose'. The relative clause further identifies the figure from Revelation.
segments.inferno-19-g118: E da le diece corna ebbe argomento continues the description: e = 'and'; da le diece corna = 'from the ten horns' (source/instrument); ebbe is the past historic of avere 'had' or 'received'; argomento here means 'argument' or 'justification/claim' (in this prophetic image, 'received support/argument from the ten horns').
segments.inferno-19-g119: Fin che = 'until' (temporal); virtute here means 'power' or 'force' (the influential power); al suo marito piacque is an impersonal/stative construction 'pleased her husband' or 'as long as power pleased her husband' — piacque is the past historic of piacere ('was pleasing'). The whole phrase means 'until such time as power pleased her husband.'
segments.inferno-19-g120: Fatto v’avete is an inverted perfect construction: fatto (past participle 'made') + v'avete (you have) = 'you have made.' V’ is the clitic vi (to/for yourselves) attached to avete; dio d’oro e d’argento = 'a god of gold and of silver' (d' = di). The line reproaches making idols of metal.
segments.inferno-19-g121: E che altro è da voi a l’idolatre is a rhetorical question: e che altro = 'and what else'; è = 'is'; da voi a l’idolatre = 'between you and the idolater' (da ... a giving the contrast). The construction asks how different you are from idolaters.
segments.inferno-19-g122: Se non ch’elli uno is elliptical: se non ch’ = 'except that'; elli (archaic form of egli) = 'he' and uno = 'one.' The speaker means 'except that he (the true God) is one' — elli is an archaic pronoun form. Note se here begins the concessive 'if not that / except that'.
segments.inferno-19-g123: E voi ne orate cento? is a sharp question: e voi = 'and you'; ne is a partitive/clitic meaning 'of them' or 'of it' (i.e. of those gods), and orate is the present you-plural of orare 'you pray' (or 'you worship'). Cento = 'a hundred.' The meaning: 'and you worship a hundred of them?'
segments.inferno-19-g124: Ahi is an interjection 'Alas' expressing lament.
segments.inferno-19-g125: Costantin is a direct address (vocative) naming Constantine; the comma indicates an apostrophe to him.
segments.inferno-19-g126: Di quanto mal fu matre is an exclamatory/elliptical phrase: di quanto = 'of how much' or 'how great'; mal = 'evil' or 'harm'; fu matre = 'was mother' meaning 'was the source' (i.e. 'was the source of much evil'). Fu is past historic of essere.
segments.inferno-19-g127: Non la tua conversion contrasts with the following clause: non = 'not'; la tua conversion = 'your conversion' (i.e. Constantine's conversion to Christianity). This line denies that the conversion was the source of the evil just mentioned.
segments.inferno-19-g128: Ma quella dote che da te prese il primo ricco patre: ma = 'but'; quella dote = 'that dowry/endowment' (i.e. the wealth or donation); che da te prese = 'which from you he took' (prese is past historic 'took'); il primo ricco patre = 'the first rich father' (likely meaning an early pope or church father who benefited). The clause blames the material gift, not the conversion, as the real source of harm.
segments.inferno-19-g86: "Di sotto al capo mio" is a prepositional phrase meaning "beneath my head" (literally "from under the head of mine"); note the archaic preposition phrase order. The verb-position inversion "son li altri tratti" places the copula before the noun phrase (poetic order): "son" (present indicative) + subject "li altri tratti" (the other figures/traits). "che precedetter me simoneggiando" is a relative clause: "precedetter" is an archaic past (preterite) meaning "preceded" and takes the object "me"; the gerund "simoneggiando" (from simoneggiare) indicates the manner/act ("committing simony") in which they preceded him.
segments.inferno-19-g87: "per le fessure de la pietra piatti" — "per" here means "through"; "de la" is the archaic form of modern "della" (of the). The adjective/participle "piatti" is placed at the end for poetic effect and agrees in number with the omitted plural referent (the "tratti" mentioned before): it means "flat/pressed flat" (i.e., lying flat against/into the fissures).
segments.inferno-19-g88: "Là giù cascherò io altresì quando verrà colui" — "Là giù" = "down there"; "cascherò" is future indicative first person singular ("I shall fall"), with the subject pronoun "io" after the verb for emphasis. "altresì" means "also/too." "quando verrà colui" introduces a time clause: "when the one (colui) comes." "ch’i’ credea che tu fossi" is a nested (reported) clause: "ch'i' credea" is a contracted form of "che io crede(a)" (I believed), and "che tu fossi" uses the imperfect subjunctive of essere to express what he believed you to be ("whom I believed you to be").
segments.inferno-19-g89: "allor ch’i’ feci ’l sùbito dimando." — "allor" = "then/at that time"; "ch'i' feci" = "that I made" (contracted "che io feci") where "feci" is passato remoto (I made). "'l sùbito dimando" is the object noun phrase "the sudden question/asking": "sùbito" here is an adjective "sudden" and "dimando" a noun "asking/question" (archaic/poetic use). The whole clause means "at the time I made that sudden question."
segments.inferno-19-g90: Ma = 'but' introducing contrast. Più è il tempo già che ... is an idiomatic inversion meaning 'it is already a longer/more time since ...' — lit. 'more is the time already that...'. I piè mi cossi: i (the) + piè (feet); mi is the first-person object (to/for me) and cossi is the past (archaic) form of cossere 'to sew/stitch', so grammatically 'I sewed (for) my feet' (i.e. 'I bound/sewed my feet'). E ch’i’ son stato così sottosopra links with che + compound tense 'son stato' (present of essere + past participle) meaning 'I have been' and the predicative adverb 'sottosopra' = 'upside down'.
segments.inferno-19-g91: Ch’el is the contraction of che + el (that he/it); non starà piantato is a future negation: starà (future of stare) + past participle piantato used adjectivally = 'will not remain planted/fixed'. Coi = con + i 'with the'; piè rossi = 'red feet', adjective ross i agreeing in number and gender with piè.
segments.inferno-19-g92: Ché here introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'because'). Dopo lui = 'after him'; verrà is future of venire 'will come'. Di più modifies the following adjective phrase to mean 'even more' or 'a more...'; laida opra = 'foul/vile work/deed' (opra = archaic for opera/operation/deed).
segments.inferno-19-g93: Di ver' ponente is a compact locution: di = 'from', ver' (aphetic form of vero) = 'true/due', ponente = 'west' — together 'from due west' or 'from the true west'. The phrase is adverbial and specifies origin/direction of the coming deed.
segments.inferno-19-g94: Un pastor = 'a shepherd' (indefinite noun phrase). Sanza is archaic for senza = 'without'; legge = 'law' (here 'without law' = lawless). The sequence is a simple noun phrase describing the coming figure.
segments.inferno-19-g95: Tal che = 'such that' (resultive conjunction). Convien is an impersonal present-tense verb meaning 'it is necessary' and governs a che + subjunctive clause; ricuopra is the (present) subjunctive 3rd-singular of ricuoprire 'to cover/cover over', so che lui e me ricuopra = 'that he cover him and me' (i.e. must cover/overwhelm both of us). Word order places the objects 'lui e me' before the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-19-g96: Nuovo is the adjective 'new' placed before the proper name for emphasis; Iasón is the proper name Jason; sarà is future of essere = 'will be'. The construction states an identity or typification in the future tense.
segments.inferno-19-g97: Di cui = 'of whom' (relative phrase referring back to Jason). Si legge is the impersonal/passive present 'one reads' / 'it is written' and ne Maccabei = 'in the Maccabees' (ne = in + the, followed by the book name), so the clause means 'of whom it is read in the Maccabees.'
segments.inferno-19-g98: E come = 'and as' (introducing a comparison). A quel is a dative 'to that one'; fu is the past (passato remoto) of essere 'was'; molle is an adjective 'soft/weak' describing the king; suo re = 'his king' (possessive + noun). The clause means 'and as to that one his king was soft/lenient.'
segments.inferno-19-g99: Così = 'thus/so'; fia is an archaic/future form meaning 'will be' (parallel to sarà); lui = 'he'; chi Francia regge is a relative clause 'who rules France' (chi = who/whoever, regge = present of reggere 'rules/governs'), so the whole means 'so will he be who rules France.'
segments.inferno-19-g100: Io non so = 'I do not know' (present tense). s’i’ is a contracted se + i’ (se io) — archaic word order for 'whether I'. mi fui is reflexive + passato remoto of essere (fui), here meaning 'I was' (the reflexive mi emphasises the subject). qui 'here' = in this situation; troppo folle is the predicate adjective 'too foolish'.
segments.inferno-19-g101: ch’i’ = che + i’ (that I) introducing the subordinate clause; pur = 'still/yet' (adverb). rispuosi is passato remoto 'I answered'. lui is the indirect object 'him'; a questo metro = 'to this metre/measure' i.e. 'to this effect' (metro literally 'metre' but here idiomatic).
segments.inferno-19-g102: Deh is an archaic interjection meaning 'ah' or 'please' used to introduce an entreaty or urgent question.
segments.inferno-19-g103: or = 'now' (adverb). mi dì is the imperative dì (from dire) with the enclitic indirect-object pronoun mi after the verb: 'tell me' (pronoun placement after the verb is normal in the imperative).
segments.inferno-19-g104: quanto tesoro = 'how much treasure' (quanto as interrogative determiner). volle is passato remoto of volere, here meaning 'demanded/required'. Nostro Segnore = 'Our Lord' (formal title). in prima = 'at first/initially'. da san Pietro = 'from Saint Peter' (da marking source/agent in older usage). ch’ei ponesse uses che + imperfect subjunctive (ponesse) after volle to express the requested/required action: 'that he should place'; le chiavi in sua balìa = 'the keys into his custody/disposal' (balìa = power/control).
segments.inferno-19-g105: Certo = 'certainly' (adverb). non chiese = passato remoto 'did not ask/ask for'. se non is an idiom meaning 'except' or 'only'. “Viemmi retro” is an archaic formula (imperative) equivalent to Latin 'Vade retro' — a curt command meaning 'get thee behind me' / 'be gone'.
segments.inferno-19-g106: Né ... né = correlative 'neither ... nor'. Pier = Saint Peter. li altri = 'the others' (li = old plural article). tolsero is passato remoto (3pl) 'took' or 'removed'. a Matia = 'from Matthias' (older uses of a before a person to mark source/recipient). oro od argento = 'gold or silver' (od is archaic form of o).
segments.inferno-19-g107: quando fu sortito = 'when he was allotted/appointed' (fu + past participle sortito, passive meaning 'was chosen'). al loco = 'to the place/office' (loco = post). che perdé l’anima ria = relative clause 'that lost his wicked soul' (perdé passato remoto 'lost'; ria = 'wicked').
segments.inferno-19-g108: Però = 'therefore/so'. ti sta is an idiomatic expression: sta (from stare) with clitic ti here means 'be (so), remain/hold your peace' or 'be satisfied/quiet' — an instruction or admonition addressed to the person.
segments.inferno-19-g109: ché = 'for/because'. tu se’ is an archaic form where se’ stands for 'sei' ('you are'). ben = 'well'; punito is past participle 'punished' used predicatively: 'you are well punished.'
segments.inferno-19-g110: e guarda ben = 'and beware/watch well' (guarda + adverb ben). la mal tolta moneta = 'the ill‑taken coin' (mal here adverbially modifies tolta — 'badly taken' = ill‑gotten). ch’esser ti fece uses che + infinitive esser + past fece (causative 'made (you) be'): literally 'which, its being so, made you [to be] ...'; ti = you (object). contra Carlo ardito = 'against bold Charles', with ardito qualifying Carlo.

sentences.inferno-19-s21: So I acted, like those people who stand there, unable to understand what is answered to them, as if abashed, and not knowing how to reply.
sentences.inferno-19-s22: Then Virgil said, “Tell him at once: ‘I am not the one, I am not the one you think.’” And I answered as I had been commanded.
sentences.inferno-19-s23: Whereupon the spirit all turned his feet aside; then, sighing and with a voice of lament, he said to me, “So, what do you ask of me?”
sentences.inferno-19-s24: If it matters to you so much to know who I am — so much that you have run along the bank for that reason — know that I was clothed in the great mantle; and truly I was a son of the she‑bear, so desirous of advancing the little bears that, for the sake of having, I put myself up and here I put myself into the purse.
sentences.inferno-19-s17: And he cried: "Are you already standing there? Are you already standing there, Boniface?"
sentences.inferno-19-s18: The writing deceived me by several years.
sentences.inferno-19-s19: Are you so quickly sated with that possession for which you did not fear to take the fair lady by deceit and then to make a torment of her?
sentences.inferno-19-s1: O Simon Magus, O wretched followers who should be the spouses of God's goodness, and you rapacious ones who have prostituted them for gold and for silver — now it is fitting that the trumpet sound for you, since you are in the third bolgia.
sentences.inferno-19-s2: We had already climbed from the rock to the next tomb, into that part which hangs exactly above the middle of the ditch.
sentences.inferno-19-s3: O highest Wisdom, how great is the art that you show in heaven, on earth and in the evil world, and how justly your power apportions!
sentences.inferno-19-s4: I saw the livid stone, along the sides and along the bottom, full of holes; each and every one was round and of one breadth.
sentences.inferno-19-s9: 'Who is that one, master, who frets himself, writhing more than his other companions,' I said, 'and into whom does a redder flame suck more?'
sentences.inferno-19-s11: And he to me: 'If you want that I take you down there along that bank which lies lowest, from him you will learn of himself and of his wrongs.'
sentences.inferno-19-s12: And I: 'That pleases me as much as it pleases you: you are lord, and you know that I do not depart from your will, and you know what is kept silent.'
sentences.inferno-19-s13: Then we reached the fourth embankment; we turned and, with weary hands, descended down there into the holed and narrow bottom.
sentences.inferno-19-s14: The good master still did not set me down from his hip, but so he brought me to the break of the one who was lamenting with his leg.
sentences.inferno-19-s15: “O whatever you are who keep the day above beneath, sad soul like a pole fastened,” I began to say, “if you can, make some reply.”
sentences.inferno-19-s16: I stood like the friar who hears the treacherous assassin's confession, who, after he is fixed, calls him back, because death ceases.
sentences.inferno-19-s5: They did not seem to me any less wide nor any smaller than those in my fair San Giovanni, made at the spot of the baptizers; one of whom — not many years ago — I broke because of one who was drowning inside: and let this be a seal that may mislead every man.
sentences.inferno-19-s6: From each mouth there jutted out — covering the feet and the legs up to the calf — the feet of a sinner, and the other stood inside.
sentences.inferno-19-s7: Both soles were alight on every one; the joints twitched so violently that, had they been broken, they would have been twisted back and left crooked.
sentences.inferno-19-s8: Just as the flaming motion of oiled things is wont to move up over the outer skin, so it was there from the heels to the tips.
sentences.inferno-19-s38: And while I was singing those notes to him, either anger or conscience that bit him was forcing him strongly with both soles.
sentences.inferno-19-s39: I firmly believe that it pleased my guide; for with so content a look he always listened to the sound of the words truly expressed.
sentences.inferno-19-s40: Therefore he took me in both his arms; and after he had me entirely up against his breast, he climbed back up by the way whence he had come down.
sentences.inferno-19-s41: Nor did he grow weary of holding me close to him; and so he carried me not so high above the crown of the arch that is spanned from the fourth to the fifth embankment.
sentences.inferno-19-s42: There he gently set down the load, gentle because of the ugly and steep crag that would be a hard passage for goats.
sentences.inferno-19-s43: Then another ravine was revealed to me.
sentences.inferno-19-s33: And if it were not that the reverence for the supreme keys, which you held in your happy life, still forbids it to me, I would use even graver words; for your greed saddens the world, trampling the good and raising the wicked.
sentences.inferno-19-s34: The Evangelist took note of you, O shepherd, when she who sits upon the waters was seen to commit harlotry with the kings; she who was born with seven heads and received argument from the ten horns, until power pleased her husband.
sentences.inferno-19-s35: You have made for yourselves a god of gold and of silver; and what else is there between you and the idolater, except that he is one and you pray a hundred of them?
sentences.inferno-19-s36: Alas, Constantine, of how much evil was mother — not your conversion, but that dower which the first rich father took from you!
sentences.inferno-19-s25: Beneath my head are the other figures who preceded me in simony, pressed flat through the cracks of the stone.
sentences.inferno-19-s26: Down there I too shall fall when that one comes whom I believed you to be, at the time I made that sudden question.
sentences.inferno-19-s27: But more time has already gone by since I sewed my feet and have been so upside down, so that he will not remain planted on his red feet; for after him there will come an even fouler deed from due west, a shepherd without law, such that it will be necessary that he cover both him and me.
sentences.inferno-19-s28: He will be a new Jason, of whom it is written in the Maccabees; and just as his king was soft toward that one, so will be he who rules France.
sentences.inferno-19-s29: I do not know whether I was too foolish here; yet I answered him to this effect: “Ah, now tell me: how much treasure did Our Lord at first demand from Saint Peter that he should place the keys in his custody?”
sentences.inferno-19-s30: He certainly asked for nothing but “Viemmi retro.”
sentences.inferno-19-s31: Neither Peter nor the others took from Matthias gold or silver when he was chosen for the post that lost his wicked soul.
sentences.inferno-19-s32: So be quiet, for you are well punished; and watch carefully the ill‑taken coin which, by making you what you are, led you to act against bold Charles.
